  1020→Level 15
  1021→
  1022→Title "Boss: The Decomposition"
  1023→
  1024→Introduction "
  1025→# Boss: Putting Navigation Together
  1026→
  1027→Time to combine everything. This boss has two parts.
  1028→
  1029→**Part 1 — The Decomposition**: Every element of `Fin 3` is
  1030→either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin 2`.
  1031→
  1032→This is the structural heart of `Fin` navigation: `Fin (n+1)`
  1033→splits into the `castSucc` images (from `Fin n`) and
  1034→`Fin.last n`. No element falls outside these two cases.
  1035→
  1036→Strategy: destructure (Level 16 of MeetFin), case-split on
  1037→the value, then use Level 12 (recognizing last) or Level 13
  1038→(finding castSucc) for each case.
  1039→
  1040→**Part 2 — Succ injectivity**: You proved this in Level 9.
  1041→Here you combine it with the decomposition in a single proof.
  1042→The pattern is the same: `ext_iff`, `val_succ`, `ext`, `omega`.
  1043→"
  1044→
  1045→/-- The last-or-castSucc decomposition, and succ injectivity. -/
  1046→Statement : (∀ i : Fin 3, i = Fin.last 2 ∨ ∃ j : Fin 2, i = j.castSucc) ∧
  1047→            (∀ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ → i = j) := by
  1048→  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  1049→  constructor
  1050→  -- Part 1: decomposition
  1051→  · Hint "Part 1: prove every `Fin 3` element is `last` or `castSucc`.
  1052→    Start with `intro i`, then destructure `i` with
  1053→    `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
  1054→    intro i
  1055→    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure: `cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
  1056→    cases i with
  1057→    | mk v hv =>
  1058→      Hint "Now `v : ℕ` and `hv : v < 3`. Case-split on `v`:
  1059→      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1060→      cases v with
  1061→      | zero =>
  1062→        Hint "Here `v = 0`. This is a `castSucc` image (value `< 2`).
  1063→        Choose `right`, then provide the preimage `⟨0, by omega⟩ : Fin 2`,
  1064→        then `ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`."
  1065→        right
  1066→        Hint "To provide the preimage, use `use ⟨i.val, proof⟩` where
  1067→        `i.val` is the value and the proof shows it's less than 2.
  1068→        Here: `use ⟨0, by omega⟩`."
  1069→        Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨0, by omega⟩`"
  1070→        use ⟨0, by omega⟩
  1071→        Hint (hidden := true) "`ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  1072→        ext
  1073→        rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
  1074→      | succ n =>
  1075→        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`:
  1076→        `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
  1077→        cases n with
  1078→        | zero =>
  1079→          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 1`. Still a `castSucc` image.
  1080→          `right; use ⟨1, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  1081→          right
  1082→          use ⟨1, by omega⟩
  1083→          ext
  1084→          rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
  1085→        | succ m =>
  1086→          Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m` once more."
  1087→          cases m with
  1088→          | zero =>
  1089→            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 2 = Fin.last 2`. Choose `left`,
  1090→            then `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]`."
  1091→            left
  1092→            ext
  1093→            rw [Fin.val_last]
  1094→          | succ k =>
  1095→            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
  1096→            Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
  1097→            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  1098→  -- Part 2: succ injectivity
  1099→  · Hint "Part 2: prove succ is injective. Start with `intro i j h`."
  1100→    intro i j h
  1101→    Hint "Convert `{h}` to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  1102→    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  1103→    Hint "Simplify succ values: `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h`.
  1104→    This gives `i.val + 1 = j.val + 1`."
  1105→    rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h
  1106→    Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, and close with `omega`."
  1107→    ext
  1108→    omega
  1109→
  1110→Conclusion "
  1111→Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Navigation**!
  1112→
  1113→**The Decomposition** (Part 1): Every element of `Fin (n+1)` is
  1114→either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`. This
  1115→is the `Fin` analog of: every natural number $k \\leq n$ either
  1116→equals $n$ or is strictly less than $n$.
  1117→
  1118→Combined with the **separation fact** from Level 7 — that no
  1119→`castSucc` image equals `Fin.last` — this gives you a **disjoint
  1120→decomposition**: `Fin (n+1)` is the disjoint union of the
  1121→`castSucc` images (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) and the singleton
  1122→`Fin.last n` (value $n$). Part 1 gives **exhaustion** (every
  1123→element falls into one of the two sets), and Level 7 gives
  1124→**disjointness** (no element falls into both). Together:
  1125→`Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) ⊔ {last}`, disjointly.
  1126→
  1127→**Connection to induction**: This decomposition is the structural
  1128→basis for **induction on Fin types**, just as `ℕ = {0} ∪ succ(ℕ)`
  1129→is the basis for induction on natural numbers. To prove a property
  1130→holds for all elements of `Fin (n+1)`, you can:
  1131→1. Handle `Fin.last n` as a base-like case
  1132→2. Handle `j.castSucc` by reducing to `Fin n` (the inductive step)
  1133→
  1134→You've already proved every ingredient for showing
  1135→`|Fin n| = n` by induction: `|Fin 0| = 0` (Fin 0 is empty,
  1136→MeetFin L13), the decomposition `Fin (n+1) = Fin n ⊔ {last}`
  1137→(disjointly), and injectivity of castSucc (Level 8). The
  1138→formal proof will appear in the Cardinality world — but the
  1139→mathematical content is already in your hands.
  1140→
  1141→There are actually *two* decompositions — the **castSucc/last**
  1142→decomposition you just proved, and the **0/succ** decomposition
  1143→from Level 10 (every element is either `0` or `j.succ`). Both
  1144→are structural tools for `Fin`. When you build tuples in the
  1145→next world: `vecSnoc` (adding at the back) uses the castSucc/last
  1146→decomposition, while `Fin.cons` (adding at the front) uses the
  1147→0/succ decomposition.
  1148→
  1149→**Succ Injectivity** (Part 2): The value reasoning pattern —
  1150→convert to values, simplify, omega — handles injectivity
  1151→automatically. This property — equal outputs imply equal inputs —
  1152→is called **injectivity**. In Lean, `Function.Injective f` means
  1153→`forall a b, f a = f b -> a = b`. You just proved
  1154→`Function.Injective Fin.castSucc` (in Level 8) and
  1155→`Function.Injective Fin.succ` (in Level 9). You'll use injectivity
  1156→formally in the FinsetImage world.
  1157→
  1158→Here's your navigation toolkit:
  1159→
  1160→| Tool | What it does | Val lemma |
  1161→|---|---|---|
  1162→| `Fin.last n` | Maximum element of `Fin (n+1)` | `Fin.val_last` |
  1163→| `i.castSucc` | Embed into larger type (same value) | `Fin.val_castSucc` |
  1164→| `i.succ` | Move forward by 1 | `Fin.val_succ` |
  1165→
  1166→And the proof recipes:
  1167→
  1168→| Recipe | Pattern |
  1169→|---|---|
  1170→| Compare `Fin` values | val lemmas + `omega` |
  1171→| Prove `Fin` equality | `ext` + val lemmas |
  1172→| Prove `Fin` inequality | `ext_iff at h` + val lemmas at `h` + `omega` |
  1173→| Prove injectivity | `ext_iff at h` + val lemmas at `h` + `ext` + `omega` or `exact h` |
  1174→| Identify as `last` | `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]` |
  1175→| Find `castSucc` preimage | `use ⟨i.val, h⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` |
  1176→
  1177→**Where this is going**: In the next world (FinTuples), you'll use
  1178→`Fin n → α` to model tuples — ordered lists of fixed length.
  1179→The `0`/`succ` decomposition powers `Fin.cons` (adding at the front),
  1180→and the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition powers `vecSnoc` (adding at
  1181→the back).
  1182→"

/-- `Fin.val_zero` states that `(0 : Fin (n + 1)).val = 0`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_zero]      -- rewrites (0 : Fin n).val to 0
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify the value of the zero element of `Fin`.
This is the dual of `Fin.val_last`: `val_last` simplifies the
maximum element's value, `val_zero` simplifies the minimum.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_zero as "Fin.val_zero" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.val_zero

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 12

Title "Recognizing the Last Element"

Introduction "
# When is an Element Fin.last?

In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images can never equal
`Fin.last`. The converse question: if you *know* an element's
value equals `n`, can you conclude it IS `Fin.last n`?

Yes. Two `Fin` elements are equal when their values are equal
(`ext` from the previous world). So if `i.val = 3` and
`(Fin.last 3).val = 3`, then `i = Fin.last 3`.

**Strategy**:
1. `intro i h` — introduce the element and hypothesis
2. `ext` — reduce `i = Fin.last 3` to `i.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
3. `rw [Fin.val_last]` — simplify RHS to `3`
4. `exact h` — the hypothesis gives `i.val = 3`
"

/-- An element with value `n` equals `Fin.last n`. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 4, i.val = 3 → i = Fin.last 3 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i h`."
  intro i h
  Hint "Use `ext` to reduce the equality to values."
  ext
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify `(Fin.last 3).val` to `3`."
  rw [Fin.val_last]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val = 3`, which is exactly `{h}`."
  exact h

Conclusion "
The pattern for recognizing `Fin.last`:
```
ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; ...
```

This converts `i = Fin.last n` into `i.val = n`, which you can
close with a hypothesis or `omega`.

In the boss, you'll use this pattern inside a case analysis: when
the case split gives you the maximum value, you'll identify the
element as `Fin.last` using exactly this recipe.

The mirror image — recognizing a `castSucc` image — is next.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 13

Title "Finding a castSucc Preimage"

Introduction "
# When is an Element a castSucc Image?

If `i : Fin 4` has `i.val < 3`, then `i` must be `j.castSucc`
for some `j : Fin 3`. Why? Because `j.castSucc.val = j.val`,
so you can build the preimage `j` using the same value: take
`j := ⟨i.val, h⟩ : Fin 3` (where `h : i.val < 3`).

This requires a creative step: you must **construct** the
preimage yourself. The witness is `⟨i.val, h⟩` — the same
number, but viewed as living in the smaller type.

**Strategy**:
1. `intro i h`
2. `use ⟨i.val, h⟩` — provide the preimage
3. `ext` — reduce to value equality
4. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` — simplify `castSucc` value
"

/-- An element with value below `n` is a `castSucc` image. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 4, i.val < 3 → ∃ j : Fin 3, i = j.castSucc := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i h`."
  intro i h
  Hint "The preimage is `⟨i.val, h⟩ : Fin 3` — the same number
  in the smaller type. Use `use ⟨i.val, h⟩`."
  use ⟨i.val, h⟩
  Hint "Now prove `i = (⟨i.val, h⟩).castSucc`. Use `ext` to
  reduce to values."
  ext
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` to simplify `castSucc.val`
  to `i.val`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]

Conclusion "
The pattern for finding a `castSucc` preimage:
```
use ⟨i.val, h⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
```

The key insight: if you know `i.val < n`, then `⟨i.val, h⟩`
is a valid element of `Fin n`, and its `castSucc` has the same
value as `i`. Since two `Fin` elements with the same value are
equal (by `ext`), you're done.

This proof move deserves a name: **value repackaging**. You take
the same underlying value but justify it in a *different type* by
providing a new bound proof. The pattern is always:
`⟨i.val, new_bound_proof⟩`. You'll see value repackaging again
in the succ direction (building `⟨v - 1, ...⟩ : Fin n` from
`⟨v, ...⟩ : Fin (n + 1)`) and in the problem set when proving
general decomposition results. In the boss, you'll use this move
inside a case analysis, where the bound comes from the case split.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 14

Title "castSucc Surjectivity"

Introduction "
# Every Non-Last Element is a castSucc Image

In Level 7, you proved that no `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`.
This level proves the **converse**: if an element is NOT `Fin.last`,
then it IS a `castSucc` image. Together, they give an exact
characterization: *an element of `Fin (n+1)` is a `castSucc`
image if and only if it is not `Fin.last n`*.

This completes the embedding picture for `castSucc`:
- **Injectivity** (Level 8): different inputs give different outputs
- **Separation** (Level 7): no output equals `Fin.last`
- **Surjectivity** (this level): every non-`last` element IS an output (converse of L7)

Together: `castSucc` is a *bijection* between `Fin n` and
`Fin (n+1) minus the last element`.

**Strategy**:
1. Derive `i.val /= n` from `i /= Fin.last n`
2. Use `omega` to get `i.val < n` (from `i.val < n + 1` and `i.val /= n`)
3. Provide the witness `(i.val, proof)` and prove equality via `ext`
"

/-- Every element not equal to `Fin.last` is a `castSucc` image. -/
Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i ≠ Fin.last n → ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i hne`."
  intro i hne
  Hint "First derive that `i.val /= n`. Use a `have` block:
  assume `i.val = n`, then show `i = Fin.last n` (contradicting `hne`)."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`have hval : i.val /= n := by`
  then inside: `intro heq; apply hne; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
  have hval : i.val ≠ n := by
    intro heq
    apply hne
    ext
    rw [Fin.val_last]
    exact heq
  Hint "Now `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) and `i.val /= n`
  together give `i.val < n`. Provide the witness."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨i.val, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  use ⟨i.val, by omega⟩
  ext
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]

Conclusion "
The surjectivity proof introduced a new technique: **deriving a
value-level fact from a Fin-level hypothesis**. You turned
`i /= Fin.last n` into `i.val /= n` by showing the contrapositive:
if the values were equal, `ext` would force the Fin elements to be
equal, contradicting the hypothesis.

This level and Level 7 together give the **if-and-only-if**:

> `i` is a `castSucc` image **iff** `i /= Fin.last n`

Level 7 proved the forward direction (castSucc images are never
last). This level proved the converse (non-last elements are always
castSucc images). Asking 'is the converse true?' is a fundamental
mathematical habit — and here the answer is yes.

With injectivity, separation, and surjectivity, the castSucc/last
decomposition is now fully justified:
- **Exhaustion**: every `i` is either `last` or `j.castSucc` (boss)
- **Disjointness**: no `castSucc` image equals `last` (Level 7)
- **Completeness**: every non-`last` element IS a `castSucc` image (this level)

In algebraic language, `castSucc` is an isomorphism between `Fin n`
and the complement of the last element in `Fin (n+1)`.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 15

Title "Boss: The Decomposition"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting Navigation Together

Time to combine everything. This boss has two parts.

**Part 1 — The Decomposition**: Every element of `Fin 3` is
either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin 2`.

This is the structural heart of `Fin` navigation: `Fin (n+1)`
splits into the `castSucc` images (from `Fin n`) and
`Fin.last n`. No element falls outside these two cases.

Strategy: destructure (Level 16 of MeetFin), case-split on
the value, then use Level 12 (recognizing last) or Level 13
(finding castSucc) for each case.

**Part 2 — Succ injectivity**: You proved this in Level 9.
Here you combine it with the decomposition in a single proof.
The pattern is the same: `ext_iff`, `val_succ`, `ext`, `omega`.
"

/-- The last-or-castSucc decomposition, and succ injectivity. -/
Statement : (∀ i : Fin 3, i = Fin.last 2 ∨ ∃ j : Fin 2, i = j.castSucc) ∧
            (∀ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ → i = j) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: decomposition
  · Hint "Part 1: prove every `Fin 3` element is `last` or `castSucc`.
    Start with `intro i`, then destructure `i` with
    `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
    intro i
    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure: `cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
    cases i with
    | mk v hv =>
      Hint "Now `v : ℕ` and `hv : v < 3`. Case-split on `v`:
      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
      cases v with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Here `v = 0`. This is a `castSucc` image (value `< 2`).
        Choose `right`, then provide the preimage `⟨0, by omega⟩ : Fin 2`,
        then `ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`."
        right
        Hint "To provide the preimage, use `use ⟨i.val, proof⟩` where
        `i.val` is the value and the proof shows it's less than 2.
        Here: `use ⟨0, by omega⟩`."
        Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨0, by omega⟩`"
        use ⟨0, by omega⟩
        Hint (hidden := true) "`ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
        ext
        rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
      | succ n =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`:
        `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
        cases n with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 1`. Still a `castSucc` image.
          `right; use ⟨1, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
          right
          use ⟨1, by omega⟩
          ext
          rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
        | succ m =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m` once more."
          cases m with
          | zero =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 2 = Fin.last 2`. Choose `left`,
            then `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]`."
            left
            ext
            rw [Fin.val_last]
          | succ k =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
            Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: succ injectivity
  · Hint "Part 2: prove succ is injective. Start with `intro i j h`."
    intro i j h
    Hint "Convert `{h}` to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
    Hint "Simplify succ values: `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h`.
    This gives `i.val + 1 = j.val + 1`."
    rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h
    Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, and close with `omega`."
    ext
    omega

Conclusion "
Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Navigation**!

**The Decomposition** (Part 1): Every element of `Fin (n+1)` is
either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`. This
is the `Fin` analog of: every natural number $k \\leq n$ either
equals $n$ or is strictly less than $n$.

Combined with the **separation fact** from Level 7 — that no
`castSucc` image equals `Fin.last` — this gives you a **disjoint
decomposition**: `Fin (n+1)` is the disjoint union of the
`castSucc` images (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) and the singleton
`Fin.last n` (value $n$). Part 1 gives **exhaustion** (every
element falls into one of the two sets), and Level 7 gives
**disjointness** (no element falls into both). Together:
`Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) ⊔ {last}`, disjointly.

**Connection to induction**: This decomposition is the structural
basis for **induction on Fin types**, just as `ℕ = {0} ∪ succ(ℕ)`
is the basis for induction on natural numbers. To prove a property
holds for all elements of `Fin (n+1)`, you can:
1. Handle `Fin.last n` as a base-like case
2. Handle `j.castSucc` by reducing to `Fin n` (the inductive step)

You've already proved every ingredient for showing
`|Fin n| = n` by induction: `|Fin 0| = 0` (Fin 0 is empty,
MeetFin L13), the decomposition `Fin (n+1) = Fin n ⊔ {last}`
(disjointly), and injectivity of castSucc (Level 8). The
formal proof will appear in the Cardinality world — but the
mathematical content is already in your hands.

There are actually *two* decompositions — the **castSucc/last**
decomposition you just proved, and the **0/succ** decomposition
from Level 10 (every element is either `0` or `j.succ`). Both
are structural tools for `Fin`. When you build tuples in the
next world: `vecSnoc` (adding at the back) uses the castSucc/last
decomposition, while `Fin.cons` (adding at the front) uses the
0/succ decomposition.

**Succ Injectivity** (Part 2): The value reasoning pattern —
convert to values, simplify, omega — handles injectivity
automatically. This property — equal outputs imply equal inputs —
is called **injectivity**. In Lean, `Function.Injective f` means
`forall a b, f a = f b -> a = b`. You just proved
`Function.Injective Fin.castSucc` (in Level 8) and
`Function.Injective Fin.succ` (in Level 9). You'll use injectivity
formally in the FinsetImage world.

Here's your navigation toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Val lemma |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.last n` | Maximum element of `Fin (n+1)` | `Fin.val_last` |
| `i.castSucc` | Embed into larger type (same value) | `Fin.val_castSucc` |
| `i.succ` | Move forward by 1 | `Fin.val_succ` |

And the proof recipes:

| Recipe | Pattern |
|---|---|
| Compare `Fin` values | val lemmas + `omega` |
| Prove `Fin` equality | `ext` + val lemmas |
| Prove `Fin` inequality | `ext_iff at h` + val lemmas at `h` + `omega` |
| Prove injectivity | `ext_iff at h` + val lemmas at `h` + `ext` + `omega` or `exact h` |
| Identify as `last` | `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]` |
| Find `castSucc` preimage | `use ⟨i.val, h⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` |

**Where this is going**: In the next world (FinTuples), you'll use
`Fin n → α` to model tuples — ordered lists of fixed length.
The `0`/`succ` decomposition powers `Fin.cons` (adding at the front),
and the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition powers `vecSnoc` (adding at
the back).
"

1→import Game.Metadata

     3→World "FinTuples"
     4→Level 1
     5→
     6→Title "Functions as Tuples"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# Functions from Fin as Tuples
    10→
    11→In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
    12→natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:
    13→
    14→**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
    15→ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.
    16→
    17→Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
    18→what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
    19→order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
    20→defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.
    21→
    22→In Lean, `![10, 20, 30]` creates a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that
    23→returns `10` at index `0`, `20` at index `1`, and `30` at index `2`.
    24→The `!` prefix distinguishes this from a plain list — it's a
    25→**vector** (a function from `Fin n`), not a linked list.
    26→
    27→**Your task**: Verify that the element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]`
    28→is `20`. (Remember: indexing starts at 0.)
    29→"
    30→
    31→/-- The element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]` is 20. -/
    32→Statement : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 20 := by
    33→  Hint "The value `![10, 20, 30] 1` reduces to `20` by definition.
    34→  Use `rfl`."
    35→  rfl
    36→
    37→Conclusion "
    38→You just accessed an element of a 3-tuple. The key idea:
    39→
    40→$$\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]}$$
    41→
    42→creates a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`. Evaluating it at index `1` gives
    43→`20` — the second element (counting from 0).
    44→
    45→This is analogous to subscript notation in mathematics:
    46→if $v = (10, 20, 30)$, then $v_1 = 20$.
    47→In Lean, `v 1` plays the role of $v_1$.
    48→
    49→Since the value is determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
    50→the equation.
    51→
    52→Notice the type: `Fin 3 → ℕ`. The domain `Fin 3` has exactly 3
    53→elements, so the function is completely determined by 3 values —
    54→it's a 3-tuple. More generally, `Fin n → α` is an `n`-tuple. The
    55→number of elements in the tuple IS the number of elements in `Fin n`.
    56→This connection between tuple length and `Fin n` cardinality will be
    57→formalized in the Cardinality world.
    58→"

    65→
    66→Title "Vec Notation with Variables"
    67→
    68→Introduction "
    69→# Tuples Work with Variables Too
    70→
    71→The `![...]` notation doesn't just work with concrete numbers.
    72→You can use variables:
    73→
    74→If `a b c : ℕ`, then `![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ` is a 3-tuple
    75→whose values are `a`, `b`, and `c`.
    76→
    77→Element access still reduces by definition:
    78→- `![a, b, c] 0 = a`
    79→- `![a, b, c] 1 = b`
    80→- `![a, b, c] 2 = c`
    81→
    82→These hold by `rfl` because `![a, b, c]` is *defined* to return
    83→`a` at index 0, `b` at index 1, and `c` at index 2 — regardless
    84→of what `a`, `b`, `c` actually are.
    85→
    86→**Your task**: Prove that the first element of `![a, b, c]` is `a`.
    87→"
    88→
    89→/-- Accessing the first element of a variable tuple. -/
    90→Statement (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by
    91→  Hint "Even with variables, element access is definitional. Use `rfl`."
    92→  rfl
    93→
    94→Conclusion "
    95→The pattern: `![a, b, c] i` reduces to the `i`-th element by
    96→definition.
    97→
    98→You can build tuples of any length and with any type:
    99→- `![5] : Fin 1 → ℕ` — a 1-tuple
   100→- `![true, false] : Fin 2 → Bool` — a Bool pair
   101→- `![1, 2, 3, 4] : Fin 4 → ℕ` — a 4-tuple
   102→
   103→The type annotation (`: Fin n → α`) is sometimes needed to
   104→help Lean figure out the types.
   105→
   106→In the next level, you'll learn to *build* tuples using
   107→pattern-matching syntax.
   108→"

   115→
   116→Title "Building Tuples"
   117→
   118→Introduction "
   119→# Building a Tuple Step by Step
   120→
   121→You've seen how to *read* tuples. Now let's *build* one.
   122→
   123→The goal asks: does there exist a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps
   124→`0` to `10`, `1` to `20`, and `2` to `30`?
   125→
   126→You already know such a function: `![10, 20, 30]`. The strategy:
   127→
   128→1. **`use ![10, 20, 30]`** — provide the witness
   129→2. **`constructor`** — split the conjunction `P ∧ Q` into two goals
   130→3. **`rfl`** — each equation holds by definition
   131→
   132→This is the same `constructor` + `rfl` pattern you'll use
   133→throughout this world whenever you need to verify properties of
   134→a specific tuple.
   135→
   136→**Your task**: Build `![10, 20, 30]` and verify its values.
   137→"
   138→
   139→/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
   140→Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
   141→  Hint "Provide the tuple with `use ![10, 20, 30]`."
   142→  use ![10, 20, 30]
   143→  Hint "The goal is now `![10, 20, 30] 0 = 10 ∧ ...`.
   144→  Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   145→  constructor
   146→  · Hint "The goal is `![10, 20, 30] 0 = 10`. Use `rfl`."
   147→    rfl
   148→  · Hint "Use `constructor` to split the remaining conjunction."
   149→    constructor
   150→    · Hint "Use `rfl`."
   151→      rfl
   152→    · Hint "Use `rfl`."
   153→      rfl
   154→
   155→Conclusion "
   156→You built a 3-tuple and verified its values step by step:
   157→
   158→1. `use` provided the witness `![10, 20, 30]`
   159→2. `constructor` split each `∧` into separate goals
   160→3. `rfl` closed each equation (the values are definitional)
   161→
   162→This `use` + `constructor` + `rfl` pattern will return
   163→throughout this world — you'll use it in the next few levels
   164→to verify properties of `Fin.cons` and `vecSnoc`.
   165→"

   169→
   170→World "FinTuples"
   171→Level 4
   172→
   173→Title "Prepending with Fin.cons"
   174→
   175→Introduction "
   176→# Fin.cons: Building Tuples One Element at a Time
   177→
   178→Under the hood, `![a, b, c]` is built using **`Fin.cons`** —
   179→a function that prepends one element to an existing tuple.
   180→
   181→`Fin.cons` takes:
   182→- An element `a : α` (the new head)
   183→- A tuple `f : Fin n → α` (the existing tail)
   184→
   185→and produces a tuple `Fin.cons a f : Fin (n + 1) → α` that
   186→starts with `a` followed by the elements of `f`.
   187→
   188→The notation `![a, b, c]` is shorthand for nested `Fin.cons`:
   189→$$\\texttt{![a, b, c]} = \\texttt{Fin.cons } a \\;\\texttt{![b, c]}$$
   190→
   191→This is the tuple analog of how `List.cons` builds lists —
   192→prepend one element at a time.
   193→
   194→**Your task**: Prove that `![a, b, c]` equals `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`.
   195→This is true by definition.
   196→"
   197→
   198→/-- `![a, b, c]` is the same as `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. -/
   199→Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
   200→    (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a ![b, c] := by
   201→  Hint "This equality holds by definition — `![a, b, c]`
   202→  IS `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. Use `rfl`."
   203→  rfl
   204→
   205→Conclusion "
   206→`Fin.cons` is the fundamental building block for tuples:
   207→- Start with the empty tuple `![] : Fin 0 → α`
   208→- Prepend `c`: `Fin.cons c ![] = ![c] : Fin 1 → α`
   209→- Prepend `b`: `Fin.cons b ![c] = ![b, c] : Fin 2 → α`
   210→- Prepend `a`: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → α`
   211→
   212→Each `Fin.cons` increases the tuple length by 1.
   213→
   214→This is the `Fin` analog of how $\\texttt{cons}$ builds linked lists.
   215→The difference: a linked list has variable length, while
   216→`Fin n → α` has the length `n` baked into the type.
   217→"
   218→
   219→/-- `Fin.cons a f` prepends element `a` to tuple `f`,
   220→producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.
   221→
   222→## Syntax
   223→```
   224→Fin.cons a f      -- explicit
   225→![a, b, c]        -- ![a, b, c] = Fin.cons a ![b, c]
   226→```
   227→
   228→## Type
   229→`Fin.cons : α → (Fin n → α) → (Fin (n + 1) → α)`
   230→
   231→## Element access
   232→- `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` (the new head) — `Fin.cons_val_zero`
   233→- `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` (the old tail) — `Fin.cons_val_succ`
   234→
   235→## Example
   236→`Fin.cons 10 ![20, 30] = ![10, 20, 30]`
   237→-/
   238→DefinitionDoc Fin.cons as "Fin.cons"
   239→
   240→NewDefinition Fin.cons

   263→**rewriting**. When a cons expression appears inside a hypothesis

   291→

   293→|---|---|
   294→| `Fin.cons_val_zero` | `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` |
   295→| `Fin.cons_val_succ` | `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` |
   296→
   297→The key skill: when you have a hypothesis like `hg : g = Fin.cons a f`,
   298→chain it with an access lemma to simplify:
   299→- `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_zero]` turns `g 0` into `a`
   300→- `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_succ]` turns `g i.succ` into `f i`
   301→

   303→levels when you prove injectivity and reconstruction properties

   339→decomposes them by dropping the first element.

   341→Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.tail f` is the

   343→
   344→$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i + 1)$$
   345→
   346→For example:
   347→$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]} = \\texttt{![20, 30]}$$

   350→`20, 30` form a shorter tuple.

   363→  rw [hf]

   365→  holds by definition. Use `rfl`."

   370→

   376→`Fin.tail f`.

   379→by a tail* — is the tuple analog of how every non-empty list
   380→is a head `::` tail. In the next level, you'll see the formal
   381→identity that ties these pieces together.
   382→"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Tuples"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

In Lean, `![10, 20, 30]` creates a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that
returns `10` at index `0`, `20` at index `1`, and `30` at index `2`.
The `!` prefix distinguishes this from a plain list — it's a
**vector** (a function from `Fin n`), not a linked list.

**Your task**: Verify that the element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]`
is `20`. (Remember: indexing starts at 0.)
"

/-- The element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]` is 20. -/
Statement : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 20 := by
  Hint "The value `![10, 20, 30] 1` reduces to `20` by definition.
  Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
You just accessed an element of a 3-tuple. The key idea:

$$\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]}$$

creates a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`. Evaluating it at index `1` gives
`20` — the second element (counting from 0).

This is analogous to subscript notation in mathematics:
if $v = (10, 20, 30)$, then $v_1 = 20$.
In Lean, `v 1` plays the role of $v_1$.

Since the value is determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
the equation.

Notice the type: `Fin 3 → ℕ`. The domain `Fin 3` has exactly 3
elements, so the function is completely determined by 3 values —
it's a 3-tuple. More generally, `Fin n → α` is an `n`-tuple. The
number of elements in the tuple IS the number of elements in `Fin n`.
This connection between tuple length and `Fin n` cardinality will be
formalized in the Cardinality world.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 2

Title "Vec Notation with Variables"

Introduction "
# Tuples Work with Variables Too

The `![...]` notation doesn't just work with concrete numbers.
You can use variables:

If `a b c : ℕ`, then `![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ` is a 3-tuple
whose values are `a`, `b`, and `c`.

Element access still reduces by definition:
- `![a, b, c] 0 = a`
- `![a, b, c] 1 = b`
- `![a, b, c] 2 = c`

These hold by `rfl` because `![a, b, c]` is *defined* to return
`a` at index 0, `b` at index 1, and `c` at index 2 — regardless
of what `a`, `b`, `c` actually are.

**Your task**: Prove that the first element of `![a, b, c]` is `a`.
"

/-- Accessing the first element of a variable tuple. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by
  Hint "Even with variables, element access is definitional. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
The pattern: `![a, b, c] i` reduces to the `i`-th element by
definition.

You can build tuples of any length and with any type:
- `![5] : Fin 1 → ℕ` — a 1-tuple
- `![true, false] : Fin 2 → Bool` — a Bool pair
- `![1, 2, 3, 4] : Fin 4 → ℕ` — a 4-tuple

The type annotation (`: Fin n → α`) is sometimes needed to
help Lean figure out the types.

In the next level, you'll learn to *build* tuples using
pattern-matching syntax.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 3

Title "Building Tuples"

Introduction "
# Building a Tuple Step by Step

You've seen how to *read* tuples. Now let's *build* one.

The goal asks: does there exist a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps
`0` to `10`, `1` to `20`, and `2` to `30`?

You already know such a function: `![10, 20, 30]`. The strategy:

1. **`use ![10, 20, 30]`** — provide the witness
2. **`constructor`** — split the conjunction `P ∧ Q` into two goals
3. **`rfl`** — each equation holds by definition

This is the same `constructor` + `rfl` pattern you'll use
throughout this world whenever you need to verify properties of
a specific tuple.

**Your task**: Build `![10, 20, 30]` and verify its values.
"

/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  Hint "Provide the tuple with `use ![10, 20, 30]`."
  use ![10, 20, 30]
  Hint "The goal is now `![10, 20, 30] 0 = 10 ∧ ...`.
  Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `![10, 20, 30] 0 = 10`. Use `rfl`."
    rfl
  · Hint "Use `constructor` to split the remaining conjunction."
    constructor
    · Hint "Use `rfl`."
      rfl
    · Hint "Use `rfl`."
      rfl

Conclusion "
You built a 3-tuple and verified its values step by step:

1. `use` provided the witness `![10, 20, 30]`
2. `constructor` split each `∧` into separate goals
3. `rfl` closed each equation (the values are definitional)

This `use` + `constructor` + `rfl` pattern will return
throughout this world — you'll use it in the next few levels
to verify properties of `Fin.cons` and `vecSnoc`.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 4

Title "Prepending with Fin.cons"

Introduction "
# Fin.cons: Building Tuples One Element at a Time

Under the hood, `![a, b, c]` is built using **`Fin.cons`** —
a function that prepends one element to an existing tuple.

`Fin.cons` takes:
- An element `a : α` (the new head)
- A tuple `f : Fin n → α` (the existing tail)

and produces a tuple `Fin.cons a f : Fin (n + 1) → α` that
starts with `a` followed by the elements of `f`.

The notation `![a, b, c]` is shorthand for nested `Fin.cons`:
$$\\texttt{![a, b, c]} = \\texttt{Fin.cons } a \\;\\texttt{![b, c]}$$

This is the tuple analog of how `List.cons` builds lists —
prepend one element at a time.

**Your task**: Prove that `![a, b, c]` equals `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`.
This is true by definition.
"

/-- `![a, b, c]` is the same as `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a ![b, c] := by
  Hint "This equality holds by definition — `![a, b, c]`
  IS `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` is the fundamental building block for tuples:
- Start with the empty tuple `![] : Fin 0 → α`
- Prepend `c`: `Fin.cons c ![] = ![c] : Fin 1 → α`
- Prepend `b`: `Fin.cons b ![c] = ![b, c] : Fin 2 → α`
- Prepend `a`: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → α`

Each `Fin.cons` increases the tuple length by 1.

This is the `Fin` analog of how $\\texttt{cons}$ builds linked lists.
The difference: a linked list has variable length, while
`Fin n → α` has the length `n` baked into the type.
"

/-- `Fin.cons a f` prepends element `a` to tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.cons a f      -- explicit
![a, b, c]        -- ![a, b, c] = Fin.cons a ![b, c]
```

## Type
`Fin.cons : α → (Fin n → α) → (Fin (n + 1) → α)`

## Element access
- `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` (the new head) — `Fin.cons_val_zero`
- `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` (the old tail) — `Fin.cons_val_succ`

## Example
`Fin.cons 10 ![20, 30] = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.cons as "Fin.cons"

NewDefinition Fin.cons

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 5

Title "Cons Access Lemmas"

Introduction "
# Rewriting with Access Lemmas

You know that `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` and `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`
hold by definition. These facts have official names:

- **`Fin.cons_val_zero`**: `Fin.cons a f 0 = a`
  — the head of a cons'd tuple is the prepended element

- **`Fin.cons_val_succ`**: `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`
  — the tail elements are the original tuple

Why name definitional equalities? Because you'll need them for
**rewriting**. When a cons expression appears inside a hypothesis
or a larger goal, `rw [Fin.cons_val_zero]` simplifies it — even
when `rfl` alone can't close the goal.

Here, you're given a tuple `g` that equals `Fin.cons a f` via a
hypothesis. Since `g` isn't literally `Fin.cons a f`, you need to
rewrite using both `hg` and the access lemmas.

**Your task**: Use `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_zero]` for the head,
and `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_succ]` for the tail.
"

/-- Access a cons'd tuple through a hypothesis. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (hg : g = Fin.cons a f) :
    g 0 = a ∧ ∀ i : Fin 2, g i.succ = f i := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction into two goals."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `g 0 = a`. Rewrite `g` using `hg`, then
    simplify with the access lemma: `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_zero]`."
    rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_zero]
  · Hint "Start with `intro i` to introduce the index."
    intro i
    Hint "Now rewrite using `hg` and the tail access lemma:
    `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_succ]`."
    rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_succ]

Conclusion "
You now have two named access lemmas in your inventory:

| Lemma | Statement |
|---|---|
| `Fin.cons_val_zero` | `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` |
| `Fin.cons_val_succ` | `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` |

The key skill: when you have a hypothesis like `hg : g = Fin.cons a f`,
chain it with an access lemma to simplify:
- `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_zero]` turns `g 0` into `a`
- `rw [hg, Fin.cons_val_succ]` turns `g i.succ` into `f i`

This **rewrite-then-simplify** pattern will be essential in later
levels when you prove injectivity and reconstruction properties
of cons'd tuples.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_val_zero a f` states that `Fin.cons a f 0 = a`.

The head of a cons'd tuple is the prepended element.

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.cons a f 0` to `a` using `rw`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_val_zero as "Fin.cons_val_zero" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.cons_val_succ a f i` states that `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`.

The later elements of a cons'd tuple come from the original tuple.

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.cons a f i.succ` to `f i` using `rw`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_val_succ as "Fin.cons_val_succ" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.cons_val_zero Fin.cons_val_succ

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 6

Title "Dropping the Head"

Introduction "
# Fin.tail: Decomposing Tuples

If `Fin.cons` builds tuples by prepending, **`Fin.tail`**
decomposes them by dropping the first element.

Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.tail f` is the
tuple `Fin n → α` that skips the first element:

$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i + 1)$$

For example:
$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]} = \\texttt{![20, 30]}$$

The first element `10` is dropped, and the remaining elements
`20, 30` form a shorter tuple.

Here you're given a function `f` that equals `![a, b, c]`.
First rewrite using the hypothesis, then the tail computes
by definition.

**Your task**: Prove that dropping the head of `f` gives `![b, c]`.
"

/-- `Fin.tail` drops the first element of a tuple. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (hf : f = ![a, b, c]) :
    Fin.tail f = ![b, c] := by
  Hint "First, substitute `f` with its known value using `rw [hf]`."
  rw [hf]
  Hint "Now the goal is `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`, which
  holds by definition. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` and `Fin.tail` are complementary operations:

- `Fin.cons` prepends: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c]`
- `Fin.tail` drops: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`

Together, they give you the head and the tail of any tuple.
The head is `f 0` (just evaluate at index 0), and the tail is
`Fin.tail f`.

This decomposition — *every non-empty tuple is a head followed
by a tail* — is the tuple analog of how every non-empty list
is a head `::` tail. In the next level, you'll see the formal
identity that ties these pieces together.
"

/-- `Fin.tail f` drops the first element of tuple `f`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.tail f    -- drop the head
```

## Type
`Fin.tail : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`

## Definition
`Fin.tail f i = f i.succ`

## Example
`Fin.tail ![10, 20, 30] = ![20, 30]`

## Key identity

400→## Key identity
   401→`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` — every tuple equals
   402→its head prepended to its tail (`Fin.cons_self_tail`).
   403→-/
   404→DefinitionDoc Fin.tail as "Fin.tail"
   405→
   406→NewDefinition Fin.tail
   407→
   408→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   409→import Game.Metadata
   410→
   411→World "FinTuples"
   412→Level 7
   413→
   414→Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"
   415→
   416→Introduction "
   417→# Every Tuple = Head + Tail
   418→
   419→You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
   420→The fundamental identity connecting them is:
   421→
   422→$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$
   423→
   424→In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
   425→prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
   426→get back `f` itself.
   427→
   428→This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
   429→
   430→This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
   431→operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
   432→and its tail.
   433→
   434→In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
   435→$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.
   436→
   437→**Your task**: Prove this identity.
   438→"
   439→
   440→/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
   441→Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
   442→    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = f := by
   443→  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states exactly this:
   444→  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
   445→  Use `exact Fin.cons_self_tail f`."
   446→  exact Fin.cons_self_tail f
   447→
   448→Conclusion "
   449→`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
   450→It says:
   451→
   452→> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
   453→> back the original tuple.
   454→
   455→But is this a genuine round-trip? You've shown that decomposing
   456→and reassembling recovers the original. In the next level, you'll
   457→prove the reverse: assembling and then decomposing also recovers
   458→the original components. Together, these make `Fin.cons` and
   459→`(head, tail)` a proper bijection.
   460→"
   461→
   462→/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
   463→`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
   464→
   465→Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

   481→import Game.Metadata
   482→
   483→World "FinTuples"
   484→Level 8
   485→
   486→Title "The Round Trip"
   487→
   488→Introduction "
   489→# Cons and Decompose Are Inverses
   490→
   491→In Level 7, you proved that decomposing a tuple into head + tail
   492→and reassembling gives back the original:
   493→
   494→$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$
   495→
   496→But a true inverse relationship goes both ways. The reverse
   497→direction asks: if you START with a head `a` and a tail `g`,
   498→cons them together, and then decompose — do you get back `a`
   499→and `g`?
   500→
   501→Concretely:
   502→- `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` — the head of the assembled tuple is `a`
   503→- `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` — the tail of the assembled tuple is `g`
   504→
   505→Together with Level 7, this shows that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)`
   506→are genuine inverse operations — a bijection between
   507→`α × (Fin n → α)` and `Fin (n + 1) → α`.
   508→
   509→**Your task**: Prove both round-trip properties. Use `rw [hg]`
   510→to substitute the hypothesis, then the access lemma or `ext`.
   511→"
   512→
   513→/-- Assembling and decomposing recovers the original components. -/
   514→Statement (a : ℕ) (g : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : Fin 3 → ℕ) (hh : h = Fin.cons a g) :
   515→    h 0 = a ∧ Fin.tail h = g := by
   516→  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   517→  constructor
   518→  · Hint "The goal is `h 0 = a`. Rewrite `h` using `hh`, then
   519→    simplify: `rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]`."
   520→    rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]
   521→  · Hint "The goal is `Fin.tail h = g`. After `rw [hh]`, the
   522→    goal becomes `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g`.
   523→    Use `ext i` to check pointwise, then `rfl`."
   524→    rw [hh]
   525→    Hint "Now use `ext i` to reduce function equality to
   526→    pointwise equality."
   527→    ext i
   528→    Hint "The goal `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) i = g i` holds
   529→    because `Fin.tail` evaluates at `i.succ`, and
   530→    `Fin.cons a g i.succ = g i` by definition. Use `rfl`."
   531→    rfl
   532→
   533→Conclusion "
   534→The round-trip is now complete. `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` are
   535→genuine inverses:
   536→
   537→| Direction | Identity |
   538→|---|---|
   539→| Decompose then reassemble | `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` |
   540→| Assemble then decompose (head) | `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` |
   541→| Assemble then decompose (tail) | `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` |
   542→
   543→This means `Fin (n + 1) → α` is in bijection with
   544→`α × (Fin n → α)` — an `(n+1)`-tuple is exactly a head
   545→element plus an `n`-tuple. This decomposition is the foundation
   546→for reasoning about tuples by induction on length.
   547→
   548→Next, you'll learn the dual construction: appending an element
   549→at the *end* of a tuple using `vecSnoc`.
   550→"
   551→

   553→import Game.Metadata

   558→Title "Appending with vecSnoc"
   559→
   560→Introduction "
   561→# vecSnoc: Adding to the End
   562→
   563→`Fin.cons` prepends an element at the *beginning* of a tuple.
   564→What about adding an element at the *end*?
   565→
   566→**`vecSnoc f x`** takes:
   567→- A tuple `f : Fin n → α`
   568→- An element `x : α`
   569→
   570→and produces `vecSnoc f x : Fin (n + 1) → α` — the tuple `f`
   571→with `x` appended at the end.
   572→
   573→The access lemmas are:
   574→- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — the last element is `x`
   575→- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — earlier elements are from `f`
   576→
   577→Notice the connection to FinNavigation: `Fin.cons` routes through
   578→`0` / `succ` (the successor decomposition), while `vecSnoc` routes
   579→through `castSucc` / `last` (the other decomposition you learned).
   580→These are **dual constructions** — one builds from the front,
   581→the other from the back.
   582→
   583→**Your task**: Prove both access lemmas for `vecSnoc`.
   584→"
   585→
   586→/-- The access lemmas for vecSnoc. -/
   587→Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
   588→    vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a ∧
   589→    ∀ i : Fin 2, vecSnoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
   590→  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   591→  constructor
   592→  · Hint "The goal is `vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a`.
   593→    Use `exact vecSnoc_last f a`."
   594→    exact vecSnoc_last f a
   595→  · Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use
   596→    `exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i`."
   597→    intro i
   598→    exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i
   599→
   600→Conclusion "
   601→You now have two ways to build tuples:
   602→
   603→| Operation | Adds where | Routes through |
   604→|---|---|---|
   605→| `Fin.cons a f` | Beginning | `0` / `succ` |
   606→| `vecSnoc f x` | End | `castSucc` / `last` |
   607→
   608→The duality is exact:
   609→- `Fin.cons` uses the **0/succ decomposition** from FinNavigation:
   610→  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `0` or `i.succ`
   611→- `vecSnoc` uses the **castSucc/last decomposition**:
   612→  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `i.castSucc` or `Fin.last n`
   613→
   614→This is the payoff from learning both decompositions in
   615→FinNavigation — they power the two dual tuple constructors.
   616→"
   617→
   618→/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
   619→producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.
   620→
   621→## Syntax
   622→```
   623→vecSnoc f x    -- append x to the end of f
   624→```
   625→
   626→## Type
   627→`vecSnoc : (Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`
   628→
   629→## Element access
   630→- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — `vecSnoc_last`
   631→- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — `vecSnoc_castSucc`
   632→
   633→## Example
   634→`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
   635→-/

   639→

   643→
   644→/-- `vecSnoc_castSucc f x i` states that `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i`.
   645→

   647→-/
   648→TheoremDoc vecSnoc_castSucc as "vecSnoc_castSucc" in "Fin"

   650→NewDefinition vecSnoc
   651→NewTheorem vecSnoc_last vecSnoc_castSucc

   653→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   654→import Game.Metadata
   655→
   656→World "FinTuples"
   657→Level 10
   658→
   659→Title "Dropping the Last Element"
   660→
   661→Introduction "
   662→# Fin.init: The Dual of Fin.tail
   663→
   664→You've seen `Fin.tail`, which drops the **first** element. Now meet
   665→its dual:
   666→
   667→**`Fin.init`** drops the **last** element.
   668→
   669→Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.init f` is a shorter
   670→tuple `Fin n → α` that keeps all elements except the last:
   671→
   672→$$\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i.\\texttt{castSucc})$$
   673→
   674→The definition uses `castSucc` — exactly the FinNavigation operation
   675→that embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` without reaching the last index.
   676→
   677→The duality:
   678→- `Fin.tail` drops the first: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`
   679→- `Fin.init` drops the last: `Fin.init ![a, b, c]` has values `a, b`
   680→
   681→**Your task**: Verify the values of `Fin.init ![a, b, c]`.
   682→"
   683→
   684→/-- `Fin.init` keeps all elements except the last. -/
   685→Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
   686→    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 0 = a ∧
   687→    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 1 = b := by
   688→  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   689→  constructor
   690→  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 0 is `![a, b, c]` at
   691→    `0.castSucc = 0`, which is `a`. Use `rfl`."
   692→    rfl
   693→  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 1 is `![a, b, c]` at
   694→    `1.castSucc = 1`, which is `b`. Use `rfl`."
   695→    rfl
   696→
   697→Conclusion "
   698→`Fin.init` and `Fin.tail` are complementary:
   699→
   700→| Operation | Drops | Keeps | Routes through |
   701→|---|---|---|---|
   702→| `Fin.tail f` | First (`f 0`) | `f 1, f 2, ..., f n` | `succ` |
   703→| `Fin.init f` | Last (`f (last n)`) | `f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)` | `castSucc` |
   704→
   705→Notice how the FinNavigation decompositions appear again:
   706→- `Fin.tail` uses **succ** (the `0`/`succ` decomposition)
   707→- `Fin.init` uses **castSucc** (the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition)
   708→
   709→In the next level, you'll see the reconstruction identity for
   710→`Fin.init` and `vecSnoc` — the dual of `Fin.cons_self_tail`.
   711→"
   712→
   713→/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.
   714→
   715→## Syntax
   716→```
   717→Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
   718→```
   719→
   720→## Type
   721→`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`
   722→
   723→## Definition
   724→`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`
   725→
   726→## Example
   727→`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.
   728→
   729→## Key identity (taught in the next level)
   730→`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` — every tuple equals
   731→its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
   732→-/
   733→DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"
   734→
   735→NewDefinition Fin.init
   736→

   738→import Game.Metadata

   740→World "FinTuples"
   741→Level 11
   742→
   743→Title "Init-Snoc Reconstruction"
   744→
   745→Introduction "
   746→# Every Tuple = Init Snoc'd with Last
   747→
   748→You know that every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail:
   749→
   750→$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$
   751→
   752→The dual identity is:
   753→
   754→$$\\texttt{vecSnoc}\\;(\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f)\\;(f\\;(\\texttt{Fin.last}\\;n)) = f \\quad (\\texttt{vecSnoc\\_self\\_init})$$
   755→
   756→In words: taking all elements except the last (init), then
   757→appending the last element (snoc), recovers the original tuple.
   758→
   759→The cons/snoc duality is now complete:
   760→
   761→| Build from | Decompose into | Reconstruct with |
   762→|---|---|---|
   763→| Front (`Fin.cons`) | head (`f 0`) + tail (`Fin.tail f`) | `Fin.cons_self_tail` |
   764→| Back (`vecSnoc`) | init (`Fin.init f`) + last (`f (Fin.last n)`) | `vecSnoc_self_init` |
   765→
   766→**Your task**: Prove the init-snoc reconstruction identity.
   767→"
   768→
   769→/-- Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element. -/
   770→Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
   771→    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last 2)) = f := by
   772→  Hint "The theorem `vecSnoc_self_init f` states exactly this:
   773→  `vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
   774→  Use `exact vecSnoc_self_init f`."
   775→  exact vecSnoc_self_init f
   776→
   777→Conclusion "
   778→The cons/snoc duality is now complete. Every tuple can be
   779→decomposed from either end:
   780→
   781→**From the front**: `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
   782→**From the back**: `f = vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n))`
   783→
   784→Both decompositions are inverse operations: decomposing and
   785→reassembling always recovers the original tuple.
   786→
   787→This is the tuple analog of a bidirectional linked list — you
   788→can traverse from the front or the back and reconstruct the
   789→whole sequence either way.
   790→"
   791→
   792→NewTheorem vecSnoc_self_init
   793→

## Key identity
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` — every tuple equals
its head prepended to its tail (`Fin.cons_self_tail`).

DefinitionDoc Fin.tail as "Fin.tail"

NewDefinition Fin.tail

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 7

Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Head + Tail

You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
The fundamental identity connecting them is:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$

In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
get back `f` itself.

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = f := by
  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states exactly this:
  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Use `exact Fin.cons_self_tail f`."
  exact Fin.cons_self_tail f

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
It says:

> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
> back the original tuple.

But is this a genuine round-trip? You've shown that decomposing
and reassembling recovers the original. In the next level, you'll
prove the reverse: assembling and then decomposing also recovers
the original components. Together, these make `Fin.cons` and
`(head, tail)` a proper bijection.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.cons_self_tail f  -- proves cons (f 0) (tail f) = f
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its first element
and the remaining elements.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_self_tail as "Fin.cons_self_tail" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.cons_self_tail

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 8

Title "The Round Trip"

Introduction "
# Cons and Decompose Are Inverses

In Level 7, you proved that decomposing a tuple into head + tail
and reassembling gives back the original:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$

But a true inverse relationship goes both ways. The reverse
direction asks: if you START with a head `a` and a tail `g`,
cons them together, and then decompose — do you get back `a`
and `g`?

Concretely:
- `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` — the head of the assembled tuple is `a`
- `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` — the tail of the assembled tuple is `g`

Together with Level 7, this shows that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)`
are genuine inverse operations — a bijection between
`α × (Fin n → α)` and `Fin (n + 1) → α`.

**Your task**: Prove both round-trip properties. Use `rw [hg]`
to substitute the hypothesis, then the access lemma or `ext`.
"

/-- Assembling and decomposing recovers the original components. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (g : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : Fin 3 → ℕ) (hh : h = Fin.cons a g) :
    h 0 = a ∧ Fin.tail h = g := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `h 0 = a`. Rewrite `h` using `hh`, then
    simplify: `rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]`."
    rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]
  · Hint "The goal is `Fin.tail h = g`. After `rw [hh]`, the
    goal becomes `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g`.
    Use `ext i` to check pointwise, then `rfl`."
    rw [hh]
    Hint "Now use `ext i` to reduce function equality to
    pointwise equality."
    ext i
    Hint "The goal `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) i = g i` holds
    because `Fin.tail` evaluates at `i.succ`, and
    `Fin.cons a g i.succ = g i` by definition. Use `rfl`."
    rfl

Conclusion "
The round-trip is now complete. `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` are
genuine inverses:

| Direction | Identity |
|---|---|
| Decompose then reassemble | `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` |
| Assemble then decompose (head) | `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` |
| Assemble then decompose (tail) | `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` |

This means `Fin (n + 1) → α` is in bijection with
`α × (Fin n → α)` — an `(n+1)`-tuple is exactly a head
element plus an `n`-tuple. This decomposition is the foundation
for reasoning about tuples by induction on length.

Next, you'll learn the dual construction: appending an element
at the *end* of a tuple using `vecSnoc`.
"

Title "Appending with vecSnoc"

Introduction "
# vecSnoc: Adding to the End

`Fin.cons` prepends an element at the *beginning* of a tuple.
What about adding an element at the *end*?

**`vecSnoc f x`** takes:
- A tuple `f : Fin n → α`
- An element `x : α`

and produces `vecSnoc f x : Fin (n + 1) → α` — the tuple `f`
with `x` appended at the end.

The access lemmas are:
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — the last element is `x`
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — earlier elements are from `f`

Notice the connection to FinNavigation: `Fin.cons` routes through
`0` / `succ` (the successor decomposition), while `vecSnoc` routes
through `castSucc` / `last` (the other decomposition you learned).
These are **dual constructions** — one builds from the front,
the other from the back.

**Your task**: Prove both access lemmas for `vecSnoc`.
"

/-- The access lemmas for vecSnoc. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a ∧
    ∀ i : Fin 2, vecSnoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a`.
    Use `exact vecSnoc_last f a`."
    exact vecSnoc_last f a
  · Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use
    `exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i`."
    intro i
    exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i

Conclusion "
You now have two ways to build tuples:

| Operation | Adds where | Routes through |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.cons a f` | Beginning | `0` / `succ` |
| `vecSnoc f x` | End | `castSucc` / `last` |

The duality is exact:
- `Fin.cons` uses the **0/succ decomposition** from FinNavigation:
  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `0` or `i.succ`
- `vecSnoc` uses the **castSucc/last decomposition**:
  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `i.castSucc` or `Fin.last n`

This is the payoff from learning both decompositions in
FinNavigation — they power the two dual tuple constructors.
"

/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

## Syntax
```
vecSnoc f x    -- append x to the end of f
```

## Type
`vecSnoc : (Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`

## Element access
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — `vecSnoc_last`
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — `vecSnoc_castSucc`

## Example
`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/

World "FinTuples"
Level 10

Title "Dropping the Last Element"

Introduction "
# Fin.init: The Dual of Fin.tail

You've seen `Fin.tail`, which drops the **first** element. Now meet
its dual:

**`Fin.init`** drops the **last** element.

Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.init f` is a shorter
tuple `Fin n → α` that keeps all elements except the last:

$$\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i.\\texttt{castSucc})$$

The definition uses `castSucc` — exactly the FinNavigation operation
that embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` without reaching the last index.

The duality:
- `Fin.tail` drops the first: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`
- `Fin.init` drops the last: `Fin.init ![a, b, c]` has values `a, b`

**Your task**: Verify the values of `Fin.init ![a, b, c]`.
"

/-- `Fin.init` keeps all elements except the last. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 0 = a ∧
    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 1 = b := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 0 is `![a, b, c]` at
    `0.castSucc = 0`, which is `a`. Use `rfl`."
    rfl
  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 1 is `![a, b, c]` at
    `1.castSucc = 1`, which is `b`. Use `rfl`."
    rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.init` and `Fin.tail` are complementary:

| Operation | Drops | Keeps | Routes through |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Fin.tail f` | First (`f 0`) | `f 1, f 2, ..., f n` | `succ` |
| `Fin.init f` | Last (`f (last n)`) | `f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)` | `castSucc` |

Notice how the FinNavigation decompositions appear again:
- `Fin.tail` uses **succ** (the `0`/`succ` decomposition)
- `Fin.init` uses **castSucc** (the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition)

In the next level, you'll see the reconstruction identity for
`Fin.init` and `vecSnoc` — the dual of `Fin.cons_self_tail`.
"

/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
```

## Type
`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`

## Definition
`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`

## Example
`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.

## Key identity (taught in the next level)
`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` — every tuple equals
its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"

NewDefinition Fin.init

World "FinTuples"
Level 11

Title "Init-Snoc Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Init Snoc'd with Last

You know that every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$

The dual identity is:

$$\\texttt{vecSnoc}\\;(\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f)\\;(f\\;(\\texttt{Fin.last}\\;n)) = f \\quad (\\texttt{vecSnoc\\_self\\_init})$$

In words: taking all elements except the last (init), then
appending the last element (snoc), recovers the original tuple.

The cons/snoc duality is now complete:

| Build from | Decompose into | Reconstruct with |
|---|---|---|
| Front (`Fin.cons`) | head (`f 0`) + tail (`Fin.tail f`) | `Fin.cons_self_tail` |
| Back (`vecSnoc`) | init (`Fin.init f`) + last (`f (Fin.last n)`) | `vecSnoc_self_init` |

**Your task**: Prove the init-snoc reconstruction identity.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last 2)) = f := by
  Hint "The theorem `vecSnoc_self_init f` states exactly this:
  `vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
  Use `exact vecSnoc_self_init f`."
  exact vecSnoc_self_init f

Conclusion "
The cons/snoc duality is now complete. Every tuple can be
decomposed from either end:

**From the front**: `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
**From the back**: `f = vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n))`

Both decompositions are inverse operations: decomposing and
reassembling always recovers the original tuple.

This is the tuple analog of a bidirectional linked list — you
can traverse from the front or the back and reconstruct the
whole sequence either way.
"

NewTheorem vecSnoc_self_init

800→Title "ext for Functions"
   801→
   802→Introduction "
   803→# Extensionality for Functions
   804→
   805→In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
   806→equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
   807→use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.
   808→
   809→For functions, extensionality says:
   810→
   811→> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.
   812→
   813→In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.
   814→
   815→When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
   816→`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.
   817→
   818→This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
   819→In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
   820→Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.
   821→
   822→**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
   823→"
   824→
   825→/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
   826→Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
   827→    f = g := by
   828→  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
   829→  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
   830→  ext i
   831→  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
   832→  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
   833→  exact h i
   834→
   835→Conclusion "
   836→The pattern is simple:
   837→
   838→1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
   839→2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`
   840→
   841→This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
   842→Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
   843→are equal when their components are equal.
   844→
   845→Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
   846→for functions. Both are valid.
   847→"
   848→
   849→/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.
   850→
   851→## For Fin elements
   852→When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
   853→`a.val = b.val` — two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
   854→natural numbers are equal.
   855→
   856→## For functions
   857→When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : α → β`, `ext i` introduces
   858→a variable `i : α` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` — two
   859→functions are equal when they agree on every input.
   860→
   861→## For finsets
   862→When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset α`, `ext x` introduces
   863→a variable `x : α` and reduces the goal to `x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ t` — two
   864→finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).
   865→
   866→All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
   867→equal when their components are equal.
   868→-/
   869→TacticDoc ext

   875→World "FinTuples"
   876→Level 13
   877→
   878→Title "Composing Tuples with Functions"
   879→
   880→Introduction "
   881→# Transforming Tuples
   882→
   883→A tuple `f : Fin 3 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
   884→A function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
   885→`g circ f : Fin 3 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
   886→to each entry.
   887→
   888→For example, if `f = ![1, 2, 3]` and `g` doubles its input,
   889→then `g circ f = ![2, 4, 6]` — applying `g` to each entry.
   890→
   891→This is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
   892→`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
   893→element of a finite set.
   894→
   895→**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
   896→both formulas, then verify each index by case split.
   897→"
   898→
   899→/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
   900→Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (g : ℕ → ℕ)
   901→    (hf : f = ![1, 2, 3]) (hg : ∀ x, g x = x * 2) :
   902→    g ∘ f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
   903→  Hint "Start with `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to reduce to pointwise equality."
   904→  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
   905→  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ⟨v, hv⟩` on the left — the
   906→  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
   907→  to unfold this to `g (f ⟨v, hv⟩)`, then substitute both
   908→  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
   909→  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
   910→  a single rewrite chain."
   911→  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
   912→  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
   913→  cases v with
   914→  | zero => rfl
   915→  | succ n =>
   916→    cases n with
   917→    | zero => rfl
   918→    | succ m =>
   919→      cases m with
   920→      | zero => rfl
   921→      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   922→
   923→Conclusion "
   924→Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
   925→`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1), g (f 2)]`.
   926→
   927→The proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
   928→an extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
   929→`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.
   930→
   931→**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
   932→function `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
   933→composition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
   934→Understanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
   935→transformations.
   936→"
   937→
   938→NewTheorem Function.comp_apply

   944→World "FinTuples"
   945→Level 14
   946→
   947→Title "Cons Injectivity"
   948→
   949→Introduction "
   950→# Fin.cons Determines Its Inputs
   951→
   952→You've learned how to *read* a cons'd tuple using the access
   953→lemmas: `Fin.cons_val_zero` and `Fin.cons_val_succ`. Now let's
   954→prove the converse: **if two cons'd tuples are equal, their
   955→heads and tails must be equal**.
   956→
   957→This is the *injectivity* of `Fin.cons`: no information is lost
   958→when you prepend. If `Fin.cons a f = Fin.cons b g`, then `a = b`
   959→and `f = g`.
   960→
   961→**New tool: `congrFun`**. If `h : f = g`, then `congrFun h a`
   962→gives you `f a = g a` — the functions agree at input `a`. This
   963→is the **pointwise extraction** move.
   964→
   965→**Strategy**:
   966→For the head: `have h0 := congrFun h 0` extracts agreement at
   967→index `0`, then simplify with `Fin.cons_val_zero`.
   968→For the tail: `ext i`, then `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`
   969→extracts agreement at `i.succ`, then simplify with
   970→`Fin.cons_val_succ`.
   971→
   972→**Your task**: Prove that `Fin.cons` is injective.
   973→"
   974→
   975→/-- If two cons'd tuples are equal, their heads and tails match. -/
   976→Statement (a b : ℕ) (f g : Fin 2 → ℕ)
   977→    (h : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons b g) :
   978→    a = b ∧ f = g := by
   979→  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into two goals: heads equal
   980→  and tails equal."
   981→  constructor
   982→  · -- heads are equal
   983→    Hint "Extract what `h` says at index `0`:
   984→    `have h0 := congrFun h 0`.
   985→    This gives `h0 : (Fin.cons a f) 0 = (Fin.cons b g) 0`."
   986→    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h0 := congrFun h 0`"
   987→    have h0 := congrFun h 0
   988→    Hint "Now simplify both sides using the access lemma:
   989→    `rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0`"
   990→    rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0
   991→    Hint "Now `{h0}` is exactly the goal. Use `exact h0`."
   992→    exact h0
   993→  · -- tails are equal
   994→    Hint "Use `ext i` to reduce function equality to pointwise
   995→    equality."
   996→    ext i
   997→    Hint "Extract agreement at index `i.succ`:
   998→    `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`."
   999→    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hi := congrFun h i.succ`"
  1000→    have hi := congrFun h i.succ
  1001→    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Fin.cons_val_succ, Fin.cons_val_succ] at hi`
  1002→    then `exact hi`"
  1003→    rw [Fin.cons_val_succ, Fin.cons_val_succ] at hi
  1004→    exact hi
  1005→
  1006→NewTheorem congrFun
  1007→
  1008→Conclusion "
  1009→`Fin.cons` is **injective**: equal outputs mean equal inputs.
  1010→The proof pattern uses **pointwise extraction** via `congrFun`:
  1011→
  1012→1. `have h0 := congrFun h 0` — extract agreement at a specific index
  1013→2. Simplify with the access lemma to relate inputs
  1014→3. For the tail, use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality first
  1015→
  1016→`congrFun` is the clean way to go from a function equality
  1017→`h : f = g` to a value equality `f a = g a` at a specific input.
  1018→
  1019→The same idea works for `vecSnoc`: if `vecSnoc f x = vecSnoc g y`,
  1020→then `x = y` (extract at `Fin.last`) and `f = g`
  1021→(extract at `i.castSucc`). You'll prove this in the next level.
  1022→
  1023→Note: the boss level tests different abilities (reconstruction
  1024→strategies and index-by-index verification), not injectivity proofs.
  1025→"
  1026→
